coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots from vitamin B1 for highly selective detection of Fe3+ ion
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Fengshou
Yang, Mengqian
Zhang, Heng
Zhu, Sizhe
Zhu, Xunjin
Wang, Kai
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are remarkable nanomaterials due to their promising excellent properties. Herein, a facile, economical and straight forward hydrothermal strategy has been developed to prepare fluorescent sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots from Vitamin B1. The as-prepared B1-CQDs have an average size of 3.2 nm and exhibit excitation-dependent PL behavior with a fluorescence quantum yield of 4.4%. Furthermore, the B1-CQDs could be applied as an efficient fluorescent probe for sensitively and selectively detection of Fe3+ with the detection limit as low as 177 nM.

Vitamin B1
Sensing
Fe3+ ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response to the Fe3+ ion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of B1-CQDs and the proposed mechanism for Fe3+ detection.
(a) TEM image and (b) corresponding size distribution histograms of B1-CQDs.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectrum of B1-CQDs.
XPS (a) survey scan, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) S 2p of B1-CQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption (black solid), excitation spectra (blue dot) and emission spectra (red dash) of B1-CQDs, (b) Fluorescence spectra of B1-CQDs with different excitation wavelengths (the right inset is the photograph of CQDs under 365 nm excitation in comparison to the blank control [left inset]). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The different PL intensity ratios (I/I0) of the B1-CQDs solutions in the presence and absence of various metal ions.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of B1-CQDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ (from 0 to 3.3 mM) (the inset shows photographs of B1-CQDs in the presence of 0 mM and 3.3 mM Fe3+ under UV irradiation); (b) the dependence of PL intensity on the concentration of Fe3+ ions within the range of 0–3.5 mM (the insert shows the linear relationship between the PL intensity and Fe3+ concentration at 0.1–1.0 mM). The excitation wavelength is fixed at 370 nm.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots from vitamin B1 for highly selective detection of Fe3+ ion

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of the Ministry of Education
School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are remarkable nanomaterials due to their promising excellent properties. Herein, a facile, economical and straight forward hydrothermal strategy has been developed to prepare fluorescent sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots from Vitamin B1. The as-prepared B1-CQDs have an average size of 3.2 nm and exhibit excitation-dependent PL behavior with a fluorescence quantum yield of 4.4%. Furthermore, the B1-CQDs could be applied as an efficient fluorescent probe for sensitively and selectively detection of Fe3+ with the detection limit as low as 177 nM.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most essential metal ions in biology, Fe3+ plays crucial roles in many physiological and pathological processes, which include deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and ribonucleic acid (RNA) synthesis, cellular metabolism, enzyme catalysis, and oxygen transport [1–4]. Thus, the precise determination of low level of Fe3+ concentration in vivo is a direct diagnosis strategy to monitor these biological pathways. Moreover, iron is among the most important sorts of contaminant in the environment. However, the current detection assays, such as spectrophotometry, atomic absorption spectrometry, and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, are limited in practical applications since they involved the sophisticated instrumentation and tedious sample preparation procedures [5–9]. Recently, the fluorescence assays have attracted considerable interest in the detection of metal ion due to their high sensitivity, great simplicity, easy monitoring, and rapid response. Therefore, it is very important and urgent to develop new fluorescent probes for iron ion with low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and high water solubility.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted a great deal of attention since they were discovered in 2004 [10]. Owing to their remarkable chemical and physical properties, such as, easy synthesis, high water solubility, stable fluorescence, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [11], they have been widely applied in bioimaging [12,13], sensor [14], photocatalysis [15], drug delivery [16], and medical diagnosis [17,18]. In the past several years, a number of synthetic ways have been developed such as hydrothermal method [19,20], microwave method [21], solid-phase method [22], electrochemical method [23], and ultrasonic treatment [24]. Moreover, the surface of carbon quantum dots is full of oxygen-containing group, which may provide CQDs with sensing properties as it could interact with metal ions strongly. However, there are few reports that using very simple method to prepare sulfur-doped CQDs with bright luminescence.

Herein, we developed a facile, economical and straight forward hydrothermal strategy to prepare fluorescent sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots from Vitamin B1. The as-prepared B1-CQDs with good water solubility exhibited excitation-dependent emission behavior with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 4.4%. Moreover, we explored the application of B1-CQDs in the detection of various metal ions. The results showed that B1-CQDs could serve as a fluorescent probe for the specific and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solution.

Thiamine hydrochloride (Vitamin B1) and ethylenediamine (EDA) were purchased from Dieckmann Corporation. All chemicals were used as received without any further purification. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer Paragon 1000 PC spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an SKL-12 spectrometer modified with a VGCLAM 4 multichannel hemispherical analyzer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a PTI QM-4/200 spectrofluorometer. The electronic absorption spectra in the UV/Vis region were recorded with a Cary UV-100 (Double Beam) UV/Vis spectrophotometer. The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) analyses were performed on an FEI-TECNAI G2 S-TWIN TEM, operating at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. X-ray powder diffraction measurements were made using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer through nonmonochromatic Al Kα X-ray at 40 kV, 40 mA.

3.37 g (0.01mol) thiamine hydrochloride (C12H18Cl2N4OS) and 2 mL ethylenediamine were dissolved in 50 mL DI water with ultrasonic mixing for 5 min, then the solution was transferred to 100 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave. After being heated at 200 °C for 12 h, the solution was cooled naturally to room temperature and centrifuged to remove agglomerated particles, and then dialyzed against DI water for three days (MWCO 3500 Da). The solid product was collected by freeze-drying and dissolved in DI water (1 mg/mL) for further applications.

The detection of Fe3+ was performed at room temperature in deionized (DI) water. For Fe3+ detection procedure, B1-CQDs aqueous solution (1 mg/mL) was diluted to 20 μg/mL, and mixed with Fe3+solution to get the final Fe3+ concentration of 0.1 mM–3.3 mM. The resulting solutions were prepared at room temperature under well-mixing conditions. The change in fluorescence intensity was recorded at the wavelength of 422 nm under the excitation wavelength at 370 nm. In addition, the selectivity of sensing system was conducted by adding 15 different kinds of metal ion at 3.3 mM into B1-CQDs solution, respectively, and the fluorescence of solution was recorded after well-mixing.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of B1-CQDs was measured according to an established procedure. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (literature quantum yield 0.54 at 360 nm) was chose as a standard. Absolute values are calculated using the standard reference sample that has a fixed and known fluorescence quantum yield. To minimize the re-absorption effects, absorbance in the 10 mm fluorescence cuvette were kept under 0.05 at the excitation wavelength (360 nm). The quantum yield of B1-CQDs was determined at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm by the following equation [13].
(1)
φ x
=
φ
Re f
(
I x
I
Re f
)
(
A
Re f
A x
)
(
n x 2
n
Re f
2
)
where φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical density. The subscript “Ref” refers to standard with known QY and “x” for the sample.

B1-CQDs were synthesized through one-step hydrothermal treatment of vitamin B1 in the presence of ethylenediamine at 200 °C for 12 h, as shown in Fig. 1 . After centrifugation and dialysis of the obtained solution, the pure product was acquired. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image in Fig. 2 a shows that the size of B1-CQDs is distributed in the range from 2.0 to 4.4 nm, with average sizes of 3.2 nm (Fig. 2b). The XRD pattern in Fig. 3 a showed a broad diffraction peak around 22.9°, which is consistent with the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials with turbostratic disorder [25].

The FTIR spectrum of B1-CQDs is shown in Fig. 3b. The band around 3430 cm−1 is assigned to O−H and N−H stretching vibration. Three absorption peaks observed at 1564, 1373 and 1094 cm−1 indicate the presence of N-H bending vibration (amine), amido C-N and amine C-N stretching bands, respectively [26]. Bands at 1138 cm−1 and 661 cm−1 correspond to C-O-C and C-S stretching vibrations, respectively. Besides, the peaks at 2926 cm−1 and 1644 cm−1 in B1-CQDs indicate the presence of C-H bond and CC stretching, respectively. XPS measurement was performed to investigate the composition of B1-CQDs. The survey XPS spectrum of CQDs in Fig. 4 a clearly shows three peaks at 284.58, 400.75, 531.34 and 163.6 eV, for C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and S 2p, respectively. The elements content of B1-CQDs was measured to be 72.28% C, 13.64% O, 10.37% N, and 3.71% S. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 4b) shows three peaks at 284.4, 285.5 and 286.4 eV, which are attributed to C-C/CC, C-S/C-N and C-O groups, respectively. In the high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 4c), the two peaks at 398.9 and 400.7 eV can be assigned to C–N–C and N-C groups. The high-resolution spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 4d) can be deconvoluted into two distinct components at 163.6 and 165.2 eV, which agree with the reported 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 positions of the –C-S-C- covalent bond of the thiophene-like S [27]. The XPS spectrum clearly demonstrated that the sulfur atom has been successfully embedded into the CQDs through the hydrothermal treatment of vitamin B1.

UV–vis absorption and emission spectra were acquired to evaluate the optical properties of B1-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the absorption spectrum of B1-CQDs exhibits a strong absorption below 350 nm with maximum peak around 320 nm, which is mainly attributed to the π−π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains [28]. Moreover, a weak absorption tail between 350 and 500 nm was also observed, which is assigned to the n−π* transitions of heteroatom (N and O)-containing bonds. The aqueous solution of B1-CQDs exhibits brilliant blue light under UV irradiation (inset in Fig. 5b). Moreover, the B1-CQDs display strong fluorescence with the maximum peak located at 422 nm when excited at 370 nm, as shown in Fig. 5b. Like CQDs reported previously [29], the B1-CQDs show a typical excitation wavelength-dependent characteristic. As shown in Fig. 5b, as the excitation wavelength gradually increases from 320 nm to 400 nm, the maximum PL wavelength of B1-CQDs shifts from 405 to 452 nm. The tunable emission of B1-CQDs was probably attributed to the different sizes of B1-CQDs or the presence of different defect states on the surfaces of B1-CQDs [30,31]. However, the exact mechanism of the excitation-dependent PL behavior remains to be established. The quantum yield of B1-CQDs was calculated to be 4.4% using the quinine sulfate as reference.

Typically, the oxygen functional groups on the surface of CQDs contribute not only to the water solubility, but also their strong interaction with metal ions. Accordingly, the impact of different metal ions including Pb2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Zn2+, NH4 +, Na+, and K+ on the PL intensity of B1-CQDs was studied. After addition of the same concentration of these metal ions respectively under well-mixing condition, the fluorescence intensities of B1-CQDs were recorded immediately. As shown in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence intensity of B1-CQDs was significantly decreased in the presence of Fe3+, while the other ions displayed weak or even negligible effects on their fluorescence intensities. These observations indicate that the fluorescence of B1-CQDs show selective response to Fe3+ and therefore could be used for the assay of Fe3+ ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response to the Fe3+ ion
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the mechanism of fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ ion and further calculate the detection limit of B1-CQDs, the quenching effect of Fe3+ ion at different concentrations was investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence intensity of B1-CQDs decreases with the increase of Fe3+ concentration from 0.1 mM to 3.3 mM. Accordingly, the emission of B1-CQDs changed from bright blue to faint blue under UV irradiation (the inset of Fig. 7a).

Fig. 7b presents the relationship between the fluorescent quenching value (ΔF = F0–F) and the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0–3.5 mM, where F0 and F indicate the fluorescence intensities of B1-CQDs at 422 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ion. The inset of Fig. 7b shows that the ΔF has a good linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0.1–1.00 mM, and the linear regression equation is ΔF = −1.70844 + 49.75735 C with a correlation coefficient of 0.999, where C is the concentration of Fe3+ (mM). The detection limit was calculated to be 177 nM from 3σ/m (where σ is the standard deviation, m is the slope of the linear response region at low concentration), which is comparable to the sensitivity reported in previous reports [28,32,33]. The high selectivity of B1-CQDs towards Fe3+could probably be attributed to the special coordination interaction between Fe3+ and the phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of B1-CQDs. Based on the previous studies of the fluorescence quenching effect of CQDs to Fe3+, we could speculate that the as-formed Fe-CQDs complexes would facilitate charge transfer and restrain exciton recombination, leading to fluorescence quenching [4,23].

In summary, we have developed a facile approach to synthesize blue-emissive B1-CQDs via the hydrothermal treatment of vitamin B1 in the presence of EDA. The as-prepared B1-CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission, which could be used as a novel fluorescent probe for the highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ via the Fe3+ induced fluorescence quenching. The detection limit was calculated to be as low as 177 nM. Altogether, these excellent photophysical properties and attractive applications indicated that B1-CQDs should be a promising candidate for sensing, biomedical diagnosis and environmental monitoring.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 21601142, 20902071), Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2017CFB689), the Wuhan Science and Technology Talent Training Program of Chenguang Project (grant no.2015070404010190), the Key Project of Scientific Research Project of Hubei Provincial Department of Education (grant no. D20141506).
